message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Measurement of post-exertion oxygen saturation has been proposed to assess illness severity in suspected COVID-19 infection. We aimed to determine the accuracy of post-exertional oxygen saturation for predicting adverse outcome in suspected COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We undertook an observational cohort study across 70 emergency departments during first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom. We collected data prospectively, using a standardised assessment form, and retrospectively, using hospital records, from patients with suspected COVID-19, and reviewed hospital records at 30 days for adverse outcome (death or receiving organ support). Patients with post-exertion oxygen saturation recorded were selected for this analysis.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We analysed data from 817 patients with post-exertion oxygen saturation recorded after excluding 54 in whom measurement appeared unfeasible. The c-statistic for post-exertion change in oxygen saturation was 0.589 (95% confidence interval 0.465 to 0.713), and the positive and negative likelihood ratios of a 3% or more desaturation were respectively 1.78 (1.25 to 2.53) and 0.67 (0.46 to 0.98). Multivariable analysis showed that post-exertion oxygen saturation was not a significant predictor of adverse outcome when baseline clinical assessment was taken into account (p=0.368). Secondary analysis excluding patients in whom post-exertion measurement appeared inappropriate resulted in a c-statistic of 0.699 (0.581 to 0.817), likelihood ratios of 1.98 (1.26 to 3.10) and 0.61 (0.35 to 1.07), and some evidence of additional prognostic value on multivariable analysis (p=0.019).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Post-exertion oxygen saturation provides modest prognostic information in the assessment of patients attending the emergency department with suspected COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    ISRCTN registry,
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="isrctn" xlink:href="56149622">ISRCTN56149622</jats:ext-link>
                    ,
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://www.isrctn.com/ISRCTN28342533">http://www.isrctn.com/ISRCTN28342533</jats:ext-link>
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key messages</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>What is already known on this subject?</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Post exertional decrease in oxygen saturation can be used to predict prognosis in chronic lung diseases</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Post exertional desaturation has been proposed as a way of predicting adverse outcome in people with suspected COVID-19</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                  <jats:p>What this study adds:</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Post-exertion oxygen saturation provides modest prognostic information in the assessment of patients attending the emergency department with suspected COVID-19</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Post-exertion oxygen saturation as a prognostic factor for adverse outcome in patients attending the emergency department with suspected COVID-19: Observational cohort study
